crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature identifies Adobe Extensible Metadata Platform (XMP) identifiers embedded within files. Defined as a standard for mapping graphical asset relationships, XMP allows for tracking of both parent-child relationships and individual revisions. There are three categories of identifiers: original document, document, and instance. Generally, XMP data is stored in XML format, updated on save/copy, and embedded within the graphical asset. These identifiers can be used to track both malicious and benign graphics within common Microsoft and Adobe document lures.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature identifies Adobe Extensible Metadata Platform (XMP) identifiers embedded within files. Defined as a standard for mapping graphical asset relationships, XMP allows for tracking of both parent-child relationships and individual revisions. There are three categories of identifiers: original document, document, and instance. Generally, XMP data is stored in XML format, updated on save/copy, and embedded within the graphical asset. These identifiers can be used to track both malicious and benign graphics within common Microsoft and Adobe document lures.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature detects the presence of a number of Windows API functionality often seen within embedded executables. When this signature alerts on an executable, it is not an indication of malicious behavior. However, if seen firing in other file types, deeper investigation may be warranted.

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Suspicious Get Local Groups Information - PowerShell
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description:
Adversaries may attempt to find local system groups and permission settings.
The knowledge of local system permission groups can help adversaries determine which groups exist and which users belong to a particular group.
Adversaries may use this information to determine which users have elevated permissions, such as the users found within the local administrators group.
Detects the use of PowerShell to identify the current logged user.

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature identifies Adobe Extensible Metadata Platform (XMP) identifiers embedded within files. Defined as a standard for mapping graphical asset relationships, XMP allows for tracking of both parent-child relationships and individual revisions. There are three categories of identifiers: original document, document, and instance. Generally, XMP data is stored in XML format, updated on save/copy, and embedded within the graphical asset. These identifiers can be used to track both malicious and benign graphics within common Microsoft and Adobe document lures.

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis:
Package: pwncat-vl (whl)
Version: 0.6.1
Description: Community-maintained fork of pwncat with Python 3.13+ support

pwncat-vl is a community-maintained fork of a post-exploitation (C2) framework designed for red team operations and penetration testing. The source code implements a comprehensive suite of offensive security features, including automated privilege escalation (exploiting vulnerabilities like CVE-2021-4034 and CVE-2025-32463), credential harvesting (SSH keys, clipboard data, and environment variables), and the ability to sideload external offensive tools such as PowerSploit and BloodHound. While the package performs actions that would be classified as malicious in a standard application—such as runtime payload downloads, shell execution via child processes, and secret exfiltration—these behaviors are the intended and documented functions of a post-exploitation tool. The logic consistently targets the remote victim machine rather than the user's local environment, and the remote downloads are directed to reputable security repositories (e.g., GitHub PowerShellMafia or BloodHoundAD).

Package: unknown (pypi)
Version: N/A
Description: N/A (no METADATA file found)

The package implements an AI-powered security engineering assistant (CIPHER) designed to provide expertise across offensive (RED), defensive (BLUE), and architectural (ARCHITECT) security domains, leveraging RAG and external LLMs (Claude, Ollama).

While the source code and embedded knowledge base contain extensive and highly sensitive material regarding offensive techniques (e.g., EDR evasion, reverse shells, AD exploits, cloud attacks), this content is consistent with the explicit purpose of the tool ('Security Engineering Assistant'). The application is designed to be transparent about its function and includes internal guardrails (e.g., strict authorization scope in the Signal bot, configuration redaction, and ethical constraints codified in the system prompt).

There is no evidence of unauthorized data collection, malicious persistence, or external C2 directed toward unknown endpoints. The use of the Anthropic SDK, ChromaDB, and Signalbot is legitimate based on the package description. The overall architecture is indicative of a professional security tool built for authorized testing and research.
